{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"•	Develop and introduce a new composite indicator “Percentage of children who received a full age-appropriate dose of vitamin A in the last 6 months” disaggregated by age group (6-59 months), and integrate it into the national DHIMS2 by March 2026 to enable routine monitoring and follow-up of vitamin A supplementation coverage in children under five. 

•	Develop and roll out a quarterly regional performance dashboard By Q1 2026, design and operationalize a quarterly automated dashboard (DHIMS2) that displays selected key nutrition indicators 

•	Establish a monthly data quality and consistency validation mechanism Starting January 2026, conduct monthly data validation exercises to compare reported rates of early initiation of breastfeeding within 1 hour” against delivery in health facility data; identify regions with >2% discrepancy, share feedback reports within 10 days, and provide targeted continuous remote and on-site support until discrepancy is reduced below 1%. 

•	Establish of a national technical working group to support mentoring by Q1 2026

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1.56,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"The presentation focuses on a trend analysis of key nutrition service utilisation indicators—specifically Vitamin A supplementation, institutional deliveries, and breastfeeding practices. These indicators are essential markers of maternal and child health, and they provide valuable insights into the performance of health systems, community-level behaviour, and overall progress toward national nutrition and health targets.

Examining utilisation trends helps us understand where improvements have been made, where progress has stalled, and what strategic actions are needed to accelerate impact

This analysis will highlight patterns over time, identify gaps in service uptake, and explore potential factors influencing these trends. Ultimately, the goal is to provide evidence that supports more targeted planning, improved programme delivery, and stronger outcomes for mothers and children.
